title: Life Will Be The Death of Me… And You Too

chapters.title: We owe a debt to the world we live in
chapters.text:
Society holds us, and we hold society. Most of us never notice that the way we treat the world around us is the way it treats us in return. Seeing your environment as broken makes it almost impossible to build anything worth building. The same goes for the people in your life. When you cannot see the good in them, doing good for them becomes a struggle.

Our lives are not separate from the world we live in. Society shapes a great deal of who we become. The more we put into it, the more we get back, which is why we have to take responsibility for our part.

Giving back is not just a feel-good idea. It builds a life that will carry you through the years. Giving back means loving people, making good memories, celebrating the people we have lost and the people we still have, and facing reality as it is.

Through Chelsea Handler's story, we will see why looking at life from more than one angle matters, and why living only for yourself can be a quiet kind of trap.

chapters.title: Comfort is not the whole story
chapters.text:
Chelsea spent a season chasing fame, money, and success. Then she woke up exhausted and ashamed by how empty it felt. At forty-two, she had no kids, no partner, and no real responsibilities. She had a Netflix show and all the freedom she could want, and she thought she had figured life out. Most of us believe the same thing, and we are wrong.

Chelsea had hoped the United States would elect its first woman president. Like half the world, she was stunned when the country went the other way. She wondered how so many people could ignore basic decency. The truth was that most of us, her included, were stuck inside a fantasy and had lost touch with the real world.

After years of comfort, Chelsea saw how entitled she had become. She was acting like the very people she claimed to dislike, and the realization stung. She had built a brand on being honest with everyone except herself. Once she saw that, she knew she had to change.

A line from Viktor Frankl's book Man’s Search for Meaning hit her hard. It said it does not matter what we expect from life, but what life expects from us. Most of us have never stopped to ask that second question. Life gets very short when we stop giving back to the world we live in.

chapters.title: Strength can hide a wound
chapters.text:
Astrology and psychics are fun, but you have to know where to draw the line between faith and reason. Meditation is a tool for finding what is wrong inside you, but people often mistake it for a religious practice, which makes it hard to share with those who do not share your beliefs. For Chelsea, religion is not the answer. She thinks religion can offer false hope, because people grab at the first sign of comfort rather than doing the harder work.

Chelsea knew she needed to talk to someone, but her pride got in the way. Like her, many of us avoid therapy because we think we are too put together to need it. She wanted help, and she was embarrassed by the wanting. For years, she told herself her life was fine and never considered talking it through.

Sometimes, we do not have the words to describe our pain, and we are afraid of being seen as anything other than strong. Because we are used to being the one who holds it all together, asking for help feels impossible. Yet we offer help to others without thinking twice. When a friend needs someone steady, we are there.

We tend to lean on our own view of the world and rarely step outside of it. That self-focused lens is part of how we got here. To grow, we have to turn that lens on ourselves.

We owe it to the people we love to take care of ourselves. If you cannot see a therapist for your own sake, do it for them.

chapters.title: Your joy cannot live in someone else
chapters.text:
Chelsea loved having her brother Chet around and assumed the feeling went both ways. She was so caught up in her own needs that she did not see how much she leaned on him. Clinging to a person's presence can be unhealthy. When your happiness depends on one person, you stop noticing that they have their own life to live.

Letting another person decide your joy quietly drains your confidence.

Tragically, Chet never came home. He had died in an accident. The loss of the person you love most takes a long time to settle in, because you do not believe it at first. Grief is brutal. The moment you think you can stand, another wave knocks you down, and the pain does not let up.

One way to soften the pain of losing a loved one is to share life with a pet, especially a dog. Studies suggest that caring for a dog can grow your compassion and round you out as a person. You cannot erase a bad memory by piling on good ones, but you can dim it until the sharp edge wears off.

Chelsea saw a string of therapists who did not help much. Then she found Dan Siegel, a neuropsychiatrist. She was ready to do the work. She wanted to learn how to forgive and how to stop getting angry at people for living differently than she did.

Siegel told her about the Enneagram, a model that says how you first reacted to leaving the comfort of the womb is how you will keep reacting for the rest of your life. You fall back on your head, your heart, or your gut. That default is where you go when life feels uncertain or uncomfortable.

chapters.title: Blame is not a cure
chapters.text:
Chelsea spent a lot of her sessions complaining about Trump, but her therapist Dan Siegel helped her see that her anger ran much deeper. She needed somewhere to put all that frustration, and Trump was the easiest target.

She was angry at herself for living in a comfortable bubble for so long. She had not seen how easy her life had been, and how hard it was for many women and people of color. Her Enneagram number, eight, leans toward anger, and anger is her default.

At first, she thought of herself as a seven, the busy, always-hunting-for-something-new type. Then she moved to eight, the powerful, take-charge type. She wanted to control every room she walked into. She was assertive, ego-driven, and heavy-handed. The hardest truth she had to face was that she had very little empathy. Knowing that gave her somewhere to start.

Chelsea was only nine when her brother died. She rarely talked about him with her old therapists, but she did with Siegel. Chet had been the first man she ever loved, the one who looked after her and who she looked up to. He died on a hiking trip after making her a promise to come home. He broke that promise, and something in her broke too.

After his death, she could not relate to how other people felt, including her own siblings. Eating cereal had been a small ritual with Chet, and watching her family keep eating it after he was gone made her angry and confused. She did not see it at the time, but she had been carrying all that anger for years.

The Enneagram has nine types: the reformer, the giver, the achiever, the individualist, the investigator, the loyalist, the enthusiast, the challenger, and the peacemaker.

chapters.title: Some events rewrite who you are
chapters.text:
After losing someone central to your life, you may never fully heal. After Chet's death, Chelsea stopped caring about her home. Her parents had been distant before the loss, and after, the gap was even wider.

Many of us deal with pain by running from it. We move away from anything that reminds us of the hurt, hoping the pain will fade. As a kid, Chelsea rode her bike whenever she was angry or close to crying. Over time, she saw that her constant motion and her love of anything new were ways of avoiding her own feelings. The bike had been a childhood escape, but it had become a pattern for her whole life. With Chet gone and her parents absent, she had to grow herself up.

You sometimes have to lose yourself to find yourself.

When she was young, she decided her family was not really hers and that she would leave as soon as she could. Later, in a moment of clarity, she saw she had been rejecting them before they could reject her. Her father had once forgotten her at the temple, so his absence was not only about Chet's death. Both parents were checked out.

In her early twenties, she started to figure out who she really was. She had become the dependable one, the fixer, the person everyone leaned on. She liked that role. Like her father, she also liked being the center of attention, with the world revolving around her. Through this work, she saw that a person can hold more than one personality at the same time.

When you meet the real you, you finally see your strengths and your weak spots side by side.

chapters.title: You are the author of your own life
chapters.text:
Chelsea saw that, in her adult relationships, she was still that same nine-year-old girl. She had reacted to Chet's death the way someone reacts to a breakup. In her mind, Chet had left her, and her father had let her go. As she grew up, she tried to test every man in her life, pushing them to prove they would not leave.

Once you know your weak spots, you can choose to work on them. That is how growth happens.

A messy moment with her dog Chunk taught her something about whether she wanted children. She had given the dog a sleeping pill meant for people, and he had a bad reaction. She left the encounter feeling like an unfit parent, and she began to wonder if she simply did not want kids.

Her mother was easygoing. Her father was strict. Her mother had let her eat candy for breakfast. After years of cancer, Chelsea's mother was on her deathbed, and Chelsea was ready to let her go. She was only sad that the rest of the family was not ready. Her relationship with her mother had shaped her, and that is why we have to be careful about the influence of parents and other people around us.

When her mother asked her to stay by her side in those last days, Chelsea swallowed her detachment. She sat with her for five days as she drifted in and out of sleep. After her mother died, Chelsea saw that staying cold toward the people you love does not pay off. It is better to look past their rough edges and pull them closer.

Treat yourself the way you treat the person you love most. Get on your own team.

chapters.title: Not everyone thinks like you
chapters.text:
Chelsea learned a lot on a trip with her cousin Molly and Molly's partner, Karen. One day, Chelsea and Karen decided to take Ayahuasca on camera for a Netflix special. In the middle of the trip, Chelsea was flooded with old memories, including a sweet one of her sister Shana from when they were little on Martha's Vineyard. The memory was good and unbroken, and Chelsea began to cry when she realized those moments had been inside her all along. Like most of us, she had been focused on the hard memories and ignoring the soft ones.

If you do not train yourself to see the good in your life, the bad memories will always lead.

A clear message came through in the trip. It was not her job to judge anyone for who they were. They had each chosen a different path, and that was fine. All she had to do was love her sister without conditions.

Chelsea had not understood why others were different from her. She had resented Shana for staying quiet when they learned that their father had a son outside his marriage. Shana's silence had made her furious, but she also liked it when Shana stood up, because that was the moment she felt closest to her. Over time, Chelsea accepted that it was fine for everyone to not be like her. Some people will always be softer and more sensitive, and that is okay. We have to be there for each other and accept people as they are. From that experience, Chelsea also accepted herself.

When you accept people as they are, you build a real community. Surround yourself with people who look after you. As a public figure, Chelsea makes sure she has people around her. In her therapy, she saw that this need came from the under-parenting she felt growing up.

chapters.title: Step out of the past to see the future
chapters.text:
While Chelsea was talking through her failed relationships, she had a sudden realization. Siegel asked her if she avoided real love because she was afraid of losing someone again. The truth of that hit her so hard she burst into tears. As much as she tried to deny it, Siegel was right. Chelsea was too afraid to take the risk.

Her whole life had been one thing after another, never sitting still. Most of us are the same. When we finally stop, we have to face the pain that has been sitting just under the surface. For Chelsea, constant movement let her avoid the grief of her childhood and the loss of her brother. When Chet died, she did her best to forget him. She pretended it had not happened. Her parents pulled away, and nobody in the house talked about their grief. In her sessions, she saw that this had become the pattern for every relationship after that. If someone did something she did not like, she walked away. Shutting down and moving on felt easier.

After her father's funeral, she saw how much there is to celebrate in a life. You can celebrate who they were and the time you had together. Chelsea had missed thirty years of celebrating Chet, and she decided not to make that mistake again.

You heal faster when you celebrate the people you loved instead of only mourning them.

In her work with Dan, Chelsea saw that she had been the difficult one all along. She had spent years disliking almost everyone, and then she realized that she was the one getting on people's nerves. Therapy is not always about speaking up more. Sometimes it is about learning to be quiet and listen. With Dan, and with her own willingness to do the work, she started to see what she had been missing. She listened more, tried to see things from other people's point of view, and recognized how lucky she was.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Chelsea's life shows that our actions follow our emotions, and that is why we live the way we do. Most of us do not see our own attitude because we think we are right. We are not always right, and life is not about us. We have to learn to live with everyone else. We shape how people see us, and we are shaped by the world around us. If we are always critical, we miss the good things that are happening right in front of us.

As much as we want to lean on other people, we have to remember that they have their own lives to live. Respect their space and their time.

The best way to find answers is to ask better questions. Most of the questions we ask ourselves are the wrong ones. Good questions do not always give us a comfortable answer. They give us the honest one, and that is how we find the real problem and start to fix it.

Try this. Chase happiness as if it is the only thing you will carry past the end. Speak up for yourself. Treat yourself with the same care you give to the person you love most.
